profile.name: Margare
profile.role_in_household: Primary client; tends to act as the household CFO and point person for investments and planning.
profile.financial_literacy_level: High—comfortable with account types (taxable vs. retirement), can follow tradeoffs (taxes, risk, liquidity), and will ask for assumptions and ranges.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Generally willing to take market risk and stay invested, but gets protective when goals are framed as 'capital preservation'—she’ll push for guardrails (cash buffer, diversification) rather than going all-in.
profile.communication_style: Direct and structured; often comes in with a mental checklist. Uses specific numbers when she’s confident, and will say things like 'I’m pretty sure' or 'ballpark' when she isn’t. Will pause to confirm definitions (gross vs. net, account ownership).
emotional_tendencies: anxious about making an avoidable mistake
profile.decision_making_style: Analytical and comparative—prefers 2–3 options with pros/cons and a recommendation; likes to sleep on big decisions but will move quickly once convinced.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open—trust increases when the advisor summarizes accurately, catches inconsistencies, and documents action items.
internal_biases: overconfidence in her ability to manage complexity
recency bias (recent market moves can influence how 'aggressive' feels in the moment)
hidden_concerns: worries about being the primary financial driver and what happens if her income changes
concerned about having enough liquid cash despite sizable investments

profile.name: Joseph
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; supportive participant who focuses on lifestyle stability and the home, and tends to defer on investment mechanics.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate—understands the big picture (income vs. expenses, mortgage payment vs. balance) but mixes up terminology and prefers plain-English explanations.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: More cautious day-to-day—prefers predictability and is sensitive to the idea of 'losing money,' even if he agrees in principle with an aggressive approach. He’ll ask 'what’s the worst case?' and gravitates to safety buckets.
profile.communication_style: Conversational and reflective; speaks in round numbers and uses qualifiers like 'I think' and 'roughly.' Occasionally interrupts with a clarifying question, especially around mortgage details or what an account 'does.'
emotional_tendencies: stress spikes when conversations get too technical
reassured by simple summaries and confirmation that bills are covered
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-driven—wants to hear Margaret’s view first, then checks that the plan feels safe and aligns with their day-to-day comfort.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Neutral-to-positive—trust builds when the advisor slows down, checks understanding, and doesn’t talk down to him.
internal_biases: status quo bias (prefers not to change accounts unless the benefit is obvious)
hidden_concerns: quietly worried about being the lower earner and whether he’s 'pulling his weight'
concerned about unexpected home-related costs and keeping monthly obligations manageable
